summary: This is a very brief teaser post directing readers to Joel's Inc. magazine column where he retells the early story of Fog Creek Software. The post opens with a satirical quote mocking dot-com era optimism about how every industry would be disrupted and startups would make everyone rich. The column apparently covers the founding narrative of Fog Creek during a turbulent period for the software industry. The post itself is too short to contain a full argument, serving primarily as a pointer to the longer Inc. piece.
key_points: The post satirizes dot-com bubble enthusiasm where every industry was supposedly going to be revolutionized by the internet
Joel frames the founding of Fog Creek Software against the backdrop of an industry upheaval or downturn
The title 'The Day My Industry Died' suggests the column covers the dot-com bust and its impact on software businesses
money_quotes: Every single industry was going to be turned upside down! New industries would be created! Start-ups would make people rich! Which is really nice, because it's awesome to be rich! And, bonus: It'll never be winter again!
key_insight: The dot-com era's boundless optimism about startups and disruption was absurdly overhyped, and building a real software company meant surviving the inevitable crash that followed.
